_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Riddle et al. Foot Ankle Int 2003 (PMID 12921358) and Hill et al. Arthritis Care Res 2008. Differentiation from plantar fasciitis per AOFAS 2010 and BMJ Clin Evid 2015. ESWT data from Speed Br J Sports Med 2014 (PMID 25148908). Surgical outcomes from Wheeler et al. Foot Ankle Surg 2014. Prevalence of asymptomatic spurs (10-27%) from radiographic surveys.

h1Title: Heel Spur: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: A heel spur is a bony outgrowth (calcaneal exostosis) on the underside of the heel bone, almost always developing where the plantar fascia attaches to the calcaneal tuberosity. Imaging surveys show heel spurs in 10-27% of adults over 40, but only a minority have any heel pain — the spur itself is usually a marker of long-standing tension at the fascia origin rather than the direct cause of pain. Patients typically present with sharp, stabbing pain on the underside of the heel during the first steps in the morning or after sitting, sometimes radiating into the arch. Untreated plantar heel pain syndromes can become chronic and disabling for years, though spurs themselves are not progressive bone-destroying lesions. More than 90% of patients improve with stretching, supportive footwear, weight management, and selective use of orthotics, NSAIDs, and shock wave therapy.

keyStats.prevalence: Radiographic heel spurs in 10-27% of adults over 40; symptomatic plantar heel pain in roughly 4-8% of the general adult population (Hill et al. 2008)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adults aged 40-65; female-to-male ratio roughly 2:1 in symptomatic cases; higher in obese and runner populations
keyStats.avgAge: Symptomatic peak 45-60 years; uncommon under 25; rises with age and BMI
keyStats.globalCases: Plantar heel pain affects approximately 10% of adults at some point in life; over 2 million US adults seek medical care for plantar heel pain each year

definition: A heel spur, or calcaneal spur (ICD-10: M77.3), is a bony outgrowth that forms on the inferior aspect of the calcaneus (heel bone), almost always at the medial calcaneal tuberosity where the plantar fascia and intrinsic foot muscles insert. Historically considered the cause of plantar heel pain, modern imaging studies and biomechanical research have clarified that the spur is a consequence of repetitive traction at the fascia origin rather than the source of pain. Heel pain in patients with spurs is most often caused by plantar fasciitis (plantar fasciopathy) — degenerative or inflammatory change in the plantar fascia itself — or by fat pad atrophy, calcaneal stress fracture, baxter neuropathy, or systemic inflammatory disease. Up to 50% of patients with plantar fasciitis have a spur, but 10-27% of asymptomatic adults also have one. Two main forms exist: inferior calcaneal spurs (under the heel, at the plantar fascia origin) and posterior calcaneal spurs (at the Achilles tendon insertion, also called Haglund deformity when combined with retrocalcaneal bursitis). Heel spurs are diagnosed on lateral foot radiographs and managed conservatively in the vast majority of cases. Podiatrists, orthopaedic foot and ankle surgeons, sports medicine physicians, rheumatologists, and physiotherapists share care. Surgery is reserved for refractory cases that have failed at least 6-12 months of well-conducted conservative management.

typesClassification.type: Inferior (plantar) calcaneal spur with plantar fasciitis
typesClassification.description: The classic form: bony outgrowth at the medial calcaneal tuberosity where the plantar fascia attaches, producing sharp underside heel pain during the first steps in the morning. The spur is associated with plantar fasciitis but is usually a marker rather than the direct cause.
Many patients have radiographic spurs without any heel pain; these are incidental findings of no clinical significance. No treatment is needed.

typesClassification.type: Posterior calcaneal spur (insertional Achilles tendinopathy or Haglund deformity)
typesClassification.description: Bony outgrowth at the Achilles tendon insertion, often associated with insertional Achilles tendinopathy, retrocalcaneal bursitis, or Haglund deformity. Pain at the back of the heel, particularly with shoes that rub the area.

Bilateral or rapidly developing heel spurs may be a manifestation of seronegative spondyloarthritis (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis). Requires rheumatological evaluation when heel spur appears in a young adult with other features of spondyloarthritis.

Coexists with obesity (BMI >30 in 70% of symptomatic cases), diabetic peripheral neuropathy, occupational standing, sports-related overuse, or pes planus/pes cavus. Each comorbidity requires specific intervention alongside spur-related care.

primarySymptoms: Sharp, stabbing pain on the underside of the heel at the medial tuberosity, classically worst during the first steps after waking or after sitting for an extended period (post-static dyskinesia).
Pain that improves after walking around for several minutes but returns or worsens with prolonged standing, walking, or running.
Dull aching pain along the arch of the foot extending from the heel forward, particularly after activity.
Tenderness on direct palpation of the medial calcaneal tuberosity at the plantar fascia origin.
Pain at the back of the heel near the Achilles tendon insertion in posterior calcaneal spur or insertional Achilles tendinopathy.
Increased pain when walking barefoot on hard surfaces, climbing stairs, or after intense exercise.
Limp or altered gait pattern with avoidance of full heel loading.
Reduced exercise tolerance and weight bearing duration as the day progresses.
Heel pain that wakes the patient at night in severe or inflammatory cases — uncommon and should prompt evaluation for other conditions.
Bilateral heel pain in a young adult — warrants screening for spondyloarthritis.
earlyWarningSigns: Sharp heel pain on the first steps in the morning lasting more than 5 minutes
Heel pain in a runner or walker who has recently increased distance or intensity
Heel pain in a patient who has recently gained weight or started occupational standing
Heel pain that has not improved with simple rest and supportive shoes after 2 weeks
Heel pain in a young adult with low-back stiffness, eye inflammation, or psoriasis suggesting spondyloarthritis
emergencySigns: Acute severe heel pain after a fall or impact — exclude calcaneal fracture with weight-bearing radiograph
Heel pain with skin breakdown, ulcer, or signs of infection in a diabetic or immunocompromised patient — exclude diabetic foot infection or osteomyelitis
Sudden inability to push off through the heel with audible pop — exclude Achilles tendon rupture
Erythema, warmth, swelling, and pain disproportionate to clinical findings — consider septic arthritis or cellulitis
Heel pain with fever, weight loss, and bilateral disease — exclude reactive arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, or osteomyelitis

causes.cause: Repetitive traction at the plantar fascia origin
causes.description: Cumulative micro-trauma at the plantar fascia attachment leads to bone formation as a reparative response. The spur develops parallel to the fascia within its insertion, not perpendicular into the soft tissue, supporting traction rather than direct compression as the mechanism.

Degenerative or inflammatory change in the plantar fascia itself is the most common cause of heel pain in patients with spurs. Histology shows fibre disorganization, chronic inflammation, and microvascular proliferation rather than acute inflammation.

BMI over 30 is present in approximately 70% of symptomatic heel spur patients; even moderate weight gain (5-10 kg) significantly raises plantar fascia load with each step. Weight loss substantially reduces symptoms.

causes.cause: Occupational standing and prolonged weight bearing
causes.description: Jobs requiring more than 4 hours of standing per day, especially on hard surfaces (factory workers, teachers, retail, healthcare workers), are a major risk factor for both plantar fasciitis and spur formation.

causes.cause: Foot biomechanics (pes planus, pes cavus, tight Achilles)
causes.description: Flat feet (pes planus) or high-arched feet (pes cavus), tight Achilles tendons, limited ankle dorsiflexion, and abnormal gait patterns all raise stress at the plantar fascia origin and the medial calcaneal tuberosity.
Worn-out shoes, lack of arch support, flat shoes, and walking barefoot on hard surfaces all raise plantar fascia load. Supportive shoes with cushioned midsoles and arch support reduce risk.

Ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease-associated arthritis cause enthesitis (inflammation at tendon and ligament insertions), producing heel spurs as part of the spondyloarthritis disease spectrum, particularly bilateral or rapidly developing spurs in young adults.

riskFactors.description: Obesity is present in roughly 70% of symptomatic heel spur patients; each unit of BMI increase raises plantar fascia load proportionately.
Symptomatic heel spurs peak in middle age as cumulative micro-trauma at the fascia origin produces bony adaptation.
Jobs requiring more than 4 hours of standing per day, especially on hard surfaces, double the risk of plantar fasciitis and associated heel spurs.
Runners (especially those increasing mileage rapidly), dancers, and jumping-sport athletes have elevated plantar fascia and heel spur risk. Gradual progression and surface variety reduce risk.

riskFactors.factor: Pes planus or pes cavus (foot architecture)
riskFactors.description: Flat or high-arched feet alter load distribution and raise plantar fascia traction. Orthotic support compensates partially.

riskFactors.factor: Tight Achilles tendon and limited ankle dorsiflexion
riskFactors.description: Limited dorsiflexion forces excessive pronation and plantar fascia loading; targeted stretching reduces symptoms.
Symptomatic heel spurs are roughly twice as common in women, partly due to footwear choices and hormonal factors.
Inflammatory enthesitis predisposes to bilateral or rapidly developing heel spurs in young adults; screen for back stiffness, eye inflammation, and skin disease.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40-65 are the typical demographic; symptomatic cases peak between 45 and 60.
Women are affected approximately twice as often as men among those seeking care.
Obese adults (BMI >30) make up roughly 70% of patients with symptomatic plantar heel pain.
Runners, especially recreational distance runners, have plantar heel pain prevalence of 5-10% annually.
Workers in jobs requiring prolonged standing have higher prevalence than sedentary workers.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is largely clinical. The hallmark history is sharp, stabbing pain on the underside of the heel during the first steps in the morning or after sitting, easing with continued movement but worsening with prolonged standing or activity. Examination begins with inspection of the standing foot for arch height, hindfoot alignment, calluses, and shoe wear pattern. Palpation localizes maximum tenderness to the medial calcaneal tuberosity in plantar fasciitis-related spurs; the windlass test (passive dorsiflexion of the great toe reproduces heel pain) supports the diagnosis. Range of motion of the ankle and subtalar joint, Achilles tendon flexibility (Silfverskiöld test), gait pattern, and neurological examination (tibial nerve, Baxter's nerve) are recorded. Lateral weight-bearing radiograph of the foot identifies the spur (typically 1-5 mm bony outgrowth at the plantar fascia origin), evaluates calcaneal pitch, excludes stress fracture, and screens for arthritis. Ultrasound is sensitive for plantar fascia thickening (>4 mm at the calcaneal insertion supports plantar fasciitis), hypoechoic changes, and fat-pad atrophy; it is increasingly used in clinic. MRI is reserved for atypical presentation, suspected stress fracture, Baxter neuropathy, or pre-operative planning. Blood tests (full blood count, CRP, ESR, HLA-B27, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP) are obtained in bilateral, persistent, or atypical cases, or when systemic features suggest spondyloarthritis. Differential diagnosis includes plantar fasciitis (often the same condition), calcaneal stress fracture, fat pad atrophy, Baxter neuropathy (entrapment of the first branch of the lateral plantar nerve), tarsal tunnel syndrome, insertional Achilles tendinopathy, retrocalcaneal bursitis, seronegative spondyloarthritis, gout, infection, and bone tumour.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical history and focused foot examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies typical post-static dyskinesia and localizes pain to the medial calcaneal tuberosity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 15-20 minute consultation including palpation, windlass test, range of motion, gait observation, and neurological screen. No special preparation needed.

diagnosticTests.test: Lateral weight-bearing radiograph of the foot
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies calcaneal spur and excludes stress fracture, arthritis, or other bone pathology
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient stands during the X-ray; takes a few minutes. Results immediate. Spurs are usually 1-5 mm bony outgrowths at the medial calcaneal tuberosity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates plantar fascia thickening, hypoechoic changes, and fat pad atrophy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless 10-15 minute clinic scan. Plantar fascia thickness above 4 mm at the calcaneal insertion supports the diagnosis of plantar fasciitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects stress fracture, Baxter neuropathy, tumour, or atypical pathology when initial assessment is unclear
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-30 minutes in the scanner. Used in refractory or atypical cases or before surgery. Results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Bloodwork (CBC, CRP, ESR, HLA-B27, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for inflammatory or systemic arthritis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within hours to days. Ordered in bilateral, young-onset, or systemically symptomatic patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies load distribution and identifies abnormal pronation, supination, or gait patterns informing orthotic prescription
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient walks on a pressure platform or with sensor insoles for 5-10 minutes. Used in selected biomechanical assessments.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is conservative in over 90% of patients and follows a stepped approach. First-line measures include patient education (mechanism, prognosis, expected timeline of 6-12 months to full resolution), activity modification (reduce running, prolonged standing, barefoot walking on hard surfaces), supportive footwear with arch support and cushioned midsole, weight loss if BMI is elevated, and a structured stretching programme targeting the plantar fascia and Achilles tendon. Stretching is the cornerstone — plantar fascia-specific stretches (Digiovanni stretch: pulling the toes back into dorsiflexion while seated) performed 3 times daily for 10 repetitions are more effective than calf stretches alone (Digiovanni et al. JBJS 2006). Pre-fabricated or custom orthotic insoles with arch support and heel cushion provide additional relief in patients with biomechanical abnormalities. NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily or naproxen 500 mg twice daily) provide short-term symptomatic relief during flares. Night splints holding the ankle in 5° dorsiflexion prevent overnight fascia shortening and reduce first-step pain in around 50% of patients. Physiotherapy with manual therapy, ultrasound, and specific exercises is helpful in moderate cases. Corticosteroid injection (40 mg methylprednisolone with local anaesthetic) under ultrasound guidance provides 6-12 weeks of relief but carries small risks of plantar fascia rupture (2-10%) and fat pad atrophy; reserved for severe symptoms not responding to first-line care. Extracorporeal shock wave therapy (ESWT) is recommended for symptoms persisting beyond 6 months, with 60-80% symptomatic improvement in randomized trials. Platelet-rich plasma injection has emerging evidence and is offered in some centres. For refractory cases (failure of 6-12 months of well-conducted conservative care), surgery — partial plantar fasciotomy, with or without heel spur resection — produces improvement in 70-80% of selected patients. Specific systemic causes (spondyloarthritis) need disease-modifying therapy under rheumatology. Diabetic foot or infected cases require multidisciplinary diabetic foot team management. Patient education on realistic timelines, gradual return to activity, and prevention of recurrence is integral.

medicalTreatments.name: Plantar fascia and Achilles stretching programme
medicalTreatments.description: Plantar fascia-specific stretches (toes pulled back into dorsiflexion while seated, holding 10 seconds × 10 reps, 3 times daily) plus calf stretches against a wall. Performed daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 70-80% within 8-12 weeks (Digiovanni et al. 2006); the most evidence-based first-line therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive footwear and pre-fabricated insoles
medicalTreatments.description: Athletic shoes with arch support and cushioned midsole; pre-fabricated orthotic insoles for arch support. Replace running shoes every 500-800 km.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined with stretching, produces relief in 60-70% of patients over 3-6 months

medicalTreatments.name: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (ibuprofen 400 mg TDS or naproxen 500 mg BD)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course (2-4 weeks) symptomatic relief during flares. Use with food; check renal function, hypertension, and gastric history.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pain scores by 30-50% in the short term; not disease-modifying

medicalTreatments.name: Night splint holding the ankle in 5° dorsiflexion
medicalTreatments.description: Worn overnight to maintain a stretched plantar fascia and Achilles tendon. Reduces post-static dyskinesia in the morning.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in first-step pain in 40-50% of compliant patients

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone 40 mg + lignocaine, ultrasound-guided)
medicalTreatments.description: Single injection at the medial calcaneal tuberosity for severe pain not responding to conservative measures. Small risk of plantar fascia rupture (2-10%) and fat pad atrophy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 6-12 weeks of significant relief in 60-70% of patients; benefit often wanes by 6 months

medicalTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shock wave therapy (ESWT)
medicalTreatments.description: Three to five sessions of focused or radial shock wave therapy at weekly intervals. Recommended for symptoms persisting beyond 6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-80% symptomatic improvement in randomized trials (Speed et al. 2014); benefits sustained at 6-12 months

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
medicalTreatments.description: Single or repeated ultrasound-guided injection of autologous concentrated platelets at the plantar fascia origin. Offered in selected centres.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Emerging evidence shows improvement comparable to or modestly better than corticosteroid at 6-12 months in chronic plantar fasciitis trials

medicalTreatments.name: Weight management with structured programme
medicalTreatments.description: Combined diet and exercise programme aiming for 5-10% body weight reduction over 6-12 months. Reduces plantar fascia load and symptom severity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Each 5 kg of weight loss reduces heel pain scores meaningfully in observational studies

surgicalOptions.name: Partial plantar fasciotomy (open or endoscopic)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical release of the medial portion of the plantar fascia for refractory plantar fasciitis. Endoscopic technique uses two small portals; open technique uses a 2-3 cm incision. Performed under local or regional anaesthesia as day case.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom improvement in 70-85% at 12 months; partial release preserves arch support and reduces lateral foot pain risk

surgicalOptions.name: Heel spur resection with plantar fasciotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Combined removal of the bony heel spur and partial fascia release for selected refractory cases. The spur itself is rarely the source of pain, so resection alone is not recommended.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable to fasciotomy alone in pooled data; preferred only when spur is unusually prominent

surgicalOptions.name: Posterior calcaneal exostectomy with retrocalcaneal bursectomy
surgicalOptions.description: For posterior calcaneal spurs (Haglund deformity) with insertional Achilles tendinopathy. Removes the bony prominence and inflamed bursa.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in 75-90% with appropriate selection

surgicalOptions.name: Gastrocnemius recession (Strayer procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: Lengthening of the medial gastrocnemius aponeurosis for patients with isolated gastrocnemius tightness causing recalcitrant plantar fascia symptoms.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70-85% in selected cohorts; allows greater ankle dorsiflexion

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and dry needling
alternativeTreatments.description: Adjunct therapy with limited but supportive evidence for short-term pain reduction in plantar fasciitis. Performed weekly for 4-6 sessions.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ice massage and contrast bathing
alternativeTreatments.description: Rolling the foot over a frozen water bottle for 10 minutes after activity reduces inflammation and provides symptomatic relief during flares.

whySeeSpecialist: A podiatrist, sports medicine physician, or orthopaedic foot and ankle surgeon assesses biomechanics, excludes alternative causes, prescribes orthotics, performs guided injections, and offers shock wave therapy or surgery in refractory cases. Rheumatology referral is warranted when bilateral or young-onset disease raises suspicion of spondyloarthritis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a clinician with experience in plantar fasciitis and heel pain syndromes, access to ultrasound for diagnosis and guided injection, ESWT or PRP availability, and links to physiotherapy with foot specialism. For surgery, look for a foot and ankle surgeon performing at least 25 fasciotomies a year.
hospitalCriteria: Podiatry or foot and ankle specialist clinic
Diagnostic ultrasound for plantar fascia assessment
Access to ESWT and PRP therapy
Foot and ankle surgical capability for refractory cases
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy weight (BMI 20-25) to reduce plantar fascia load.
Wear supportive footwear with arch support and cushioned midsole; replace running shoes every 500-800 km.
Stretch the plantar fascia and Achilles tendon daily, especially before exercise.
Increase running mileage or new walking activity gradually (10% per week rule).
Use anti-fatigue mats and supportive shoes for occupational standing.
Avoid prolonged barefoot walking on hard surfaces, particularly in obese individuals or those with foot deformities.
Address pes planus or pes cavus with appropriate orthotic support.
Treat underlying inflammatory arthritis with disease-modifying therapy.
lifestyleModifications: Perform daily plantar fascia and calf stretching (3 sets of 10 repetitions).
Wear supportive shoes throughout the day; avoid walking barefoot at home.
Reduce or modify high-impact activities (running, jumping) until symptoms resolve.
Lose weight if BMI is over 25 through diet and lower-impact exercise (swimming, cycling).
Use ice massage or contrast bathing after activity during symptomatic phases.
Wear a night splint if morning pain is severe.
Maintain regular exercise but switch to lower-impact options during flares.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in fruit, vegetables, oily fish, olive oil, and whole grains for weight management and anti-inflammatory effect
Adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support muscle tone in the foot intrinsics
Calcium 1,000 mg/day and vitamin D 800-1,000 IU/day for bone health
Hydration (1.5-2 L/day) to maintain soft tissue elasticity
avoid: Excess calories that contribute to weight gain
High alcohol consumption that may increase gout risk and impair tissue repair
Pro-inflammatory ultra-processed foods, refined sugars, and trans fats

exerciseGuidelines: Encourage low-impact aerobic exercise (swimming, cycling, elliptical) during symptomatic phases. Continue stretching daily. Gradually reintroduce walking and running over 4-6 weeks as symptoms allow. Avoid sudden mileage increases; use the 10%-per-week rule. Strengthening of intrinsic foot muscles (towel scrunches, toe pickups) improves arch support.
dailyManagement: Perform plantar fascia and Achilles stretches first thing in the morning before bearing weight.
Wear supportive shoes with arch support at home and outside.
Use cushioned heel inserts or pre-fabricated orthotic insoles in all shoes.
Apply ice for 10 minutes after standing or activity.
Take prescribed NSAIDs with food during flares.
Track pain scores and activity levels in a simple diary.
prognosis: Outlook is generally favourable. Around 80-90% of patients improve substantially with conservative care over 6-12 months. Symptoms typically resolve within 6 months with consistent stretching, supportive footwear, weight management, and time. About 10-20% have chronic symptoms persisting beyond a year and may benefit from advanced therapies (ESWT, PRP) or surgery. Heel spur surgery (plantar fasciotomy) achieves 70-85% symptom improvement in carefully selected patients with prolonged failure of conservative care. Recurrence is common (20-30%) if predisposing factors (obesity, occupational standing, biomechanical abnormalities) are not addressed. Severe long-term disability is rare; major complications of treatment include fascia rupture (2-10% after steroid injection) and persistent post-surgical pain or lateral foot strain after extensive release. Quality of life is significantly affected during symptomatic phases but returns to near-normal in most patients after resolution.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients notice improvement with stretching and supportive footwear within 4-8 weeks. Maximum improvement from conservative care typically occurs over 6-12 months. ESWT effects appear over 8-12 weeks. Post-fasciotomy surgery: weight bearing in supportive boot within 1 week, full weight bearing at 4 weeks, return to running at 8-12 weeks, full activity at 4-6 months.
complications: Chronic plantar fasciitis with persistent symptoms beyond 12 months — requires advanced therapy (ESWT, PRP, surgery).
Plantar fascia rupture (2-10% after corticosteroid injection) — produces acute worsening pain and loss of arch support.
Fat pad atrophy from repeated corticosteroid injection — causes bruising sensation under the heel; difficult to reverse.
Heel pad neuropathy (Baxter nerve entrapment) — may coexist with or be confused for plantar fasciitis; needs diagnostic injection or surgical release.
Calcaneal stress fracture in repetitive impact athletes — confirmed by MRI; managed with non-weight bearing.
Altered gait pattern leading to knee, hip, or back pain — addressed by physiotherapy and biomechanical correction.
Persistent post-surgical pain or lateral column overload after extensive fascia release.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Plantar Fasciitis and Bone Spurs
supportResources.description: Patient-friendly resource covering plantar fasciitis, heel spurs, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association (APMA)
supportResources.description: US podiatry professional body with patient resources on heel pain and foot health.

supportResources.name: NHS — Heel pain
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service plain-language patient guide to heel pain causes and self-care.

supportResources.name: British Orthopaedic Foot and Ankle Society (BOFAS) — Patient leaflets
supportResources.description: Patient leaflets on plantar fasciitis, heel spurs, and surgical options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis is the same clinical entity in most cases; a heel spur is often an associated radiographic finding rather than the actual cause of pain. Treatment is functionally identical, focused on the plantar fascia rather than the spur itself.

confusedWithConditions.name: Achilles Tendinopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achilles tendinopathy produces pain at the back of the heel near the Achilles tendon insertion or 2-6 cm above it, with tenderness on palpation along the tendon. Plantar heel spur pain is under the heel at the plantar fascia origin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis or Spondyloarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Inflammatory arthritis produces bilateral, multiple-joint symptoms with morning stiffness, raised inflammatory markers, and systemic features. Bilateral heel spurs in a young adult warrant rheumatology referral to screen for ankylosing spondylitis or psoriatic arthritis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Subtalar or Mid-foot Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Foot OA produces deeper joint pain in the subtalar or mid-foot, worse with activity and improved with rest, and shows joint-space narrowing on X-ray. Heel spur pain localizes to the underside of the heel and responds to plantar fascia treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Most heel spurs occur alongside plantar fasciitis; treatment is largely identical and focused on the fascia rather than the bony spur.

relatedConditions.name: Achilles Tendinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Posterior heel spurs at the Achilles insertion produce insertional Achilles tendinopathy; managed similarly with stretching, eccentric loading, and selective surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Inflammatory Arthritis / Spondyloarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bilateral or young-onset heel spurs may be a feature of seronegative spondyloarthritis (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic, reactive); requires rheumatological assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Subtalar and mid-foot OA can coexist with heel spurs and contribute to overall foot pain; X-ray distinguishes joint-space narrowing from spur formation.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Widespread chronic pain syndromes can include heel pain; multidisciplinary care needed when local treatments fail.

faqs.question: What is a heel spur?
faqs.answer: A heel spur is a bony outgrowth on the underside or back of the heel bone, almost always at the plantar fascia or Achilles tendon insertion. It develops in response to repetitive traction over many years. The spur itself is usually painless; pain is typically from plantar fasciitis or insertional Achilles tendinopathy.

faqs.question: Is a heel spur the same as plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: Not exactly. A heel spur is a bony outgrowth often seen on X-ray, while plantar fasciitis is inflammation or degeneration of the plantar fascia itself. They commonly occur together — up to 50% of plantar fasciitis patients have a heel spur — but the spur is usually a marker rather than the cause of pain.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a heel spur?
faqs.answer: Typical symptoms are sharp stabbing pain on the underside of the heel during the first steps in the morning or after sitting, easing with continued walking and worsening with prolonged standing or running. Tenderness at the medial calcaneal tuberosity is characteristic. Many heel spurs are asymptomatic and detected incidentally on X-ray.

faqs.question: What causes heel spurs?
faqs.answer: Heel spurs result from repetitive traction at the plantar fascia or Achilles tendon insertion, often associated with obesity, prolonged standing, repetitive running, tight calf muscles, abnormal foot shape, and inappropriate footwear. Seronegative spondyloarthritis can cause inflammatory heel spurs in young adults.

faqs.question: How is a heel spur diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, supported by a lateral weight-bearing foot X-ray showing the bony outgrowth. Ultrasound is useful to confirm plantar fascia thickening. MRI is reserved for atypical or refractory cases. Bloodwork screens for inflammatory arthritis in bilateral or young-onset disease.

faqs.question: How is a heel spur treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is plantar fascia and Achilles stretching, supportive footwear, weight loss, activity modification, NSAIDs, and night splints. Corticosteroid injection or extracorporeal shock wave therapy is offered for refractory symptoms. Surgery (plantar fasciotomy) is reserved for cases failing 6-12 months of conservative care.

faqs.question: Do heel spurs need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is rarely needed. Over 90% of patients improve with stretching, supportive footwear, and time. Surgery is reserved for refractory cases that have failed at least 6-12 months of comprehensive conservative care. The most common procedure is partial plantar fasciotomy, sometimes combined with spur resection.

faqs.question: Can heel spurs be cured?
faqs.answer: The bony spur itself usually persists, but the pain associated with heel spurs and plantar fasciitis resolves in 80-90% of patients with appropriate stretching, footwear, and weight management over 6-12 months. Recurrence is common if predisposing factors are not addressed.

faqs.question: What shoes are best for heel spurs?
faqs.answer: Choose shoes with cushioned midsoles, firm heel counters, arch support, and a slight heel rise (1-2 cm). Replace running shoes every 500-800 km. Avoid flat shoes, worn-out shoes, and prolonged barefoot walking. Pre-fabricated or custom orthotics add further support.

faqs.question: How long does heel spur pain last?
faqs.answer: Most patients improve substantially within 6-12 months of starting consistent stretching, supportive footwear, and weight management. About 10-20% have symptoms beyond a year and may need advanced therapies. Acute flares typically settle within 4-8 weeks with appropriate care.

faqs.question: Can I run with a heel spur?
faqs.answer: Light running may be possible if symptoms allow, but reducing impact during symptomatic phases is wise. Switch to low-impact alternatives (cycling, swimming, elliptical) until symptoms settle, then progress mileage by no more than 10% per week. Use supportive shoes and stretch before and after activity.

faqs.question: Are steroid injections helpful for heel spurs?
faqs.answer: Corticosteroid injection at the plantar fascia origin provides 6-12 weeks of relief in 60-70% of patients with severe symptoms. Repeated injections carry a 2-10% risk of plantar fascia rupture and fat pad atrophy, so use is selective and limited.

faqs.question: What is shock wave therapy?
faqs.answer: Extracorporeal shock wave therapy (ESWT) delivers focused acoustic pulses to the plantar fascia origin. Three to five sessions at weekly intervals achieve 60-80% symptom improvement in randomized trials. It is recommended for symptoms persisting beyond 6 months and is offered in specialist clinics.

faqs.question: Will my heel spur disappear with treatment?
faqs.answer: The bony spur itself typically persists even after symptoms resolve. The aim of treatment is to relieve the plantar fasciitis and traction strain rather than dissolve the bone. Recurrence of symptoms can occur if predisposing biomechanical or weight factors remain unaddressed.

faqs.question: Can children get heel spurs?
faqs.answer: True bony heel spurs are uncommon in children. Children with heel pain usually have calcaneal apophysitis (Sever disease), an overuse injury at the growth plate. It resolves with rest, stretching, heel cushions, and growth plate maturation.

faqs.question: Does weight loss help heel pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Weight loss reduces plantar fascia load with every step and is one of the most effective interventions for symptomatic plantar fasciitis associated with heel spurs. A 5-10% body weight reduction over 6-12 months produces meaningful improvement in heel pain.

faqs.question: Are heel spurs hereditary?
faqs.answer: Foot shape (pes planus, pes cavus) has a heritable component, and inflammatory arthritis associated with heel spurs (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis) has genetic predisposition. The spurs themselves are biomechanically driven rather than directly inherited.

faqs.question: What is the windlass test?
faqs.answer: The windlass test is a clinical manoeuvre in which the examiner passively dorsiflexes the patient's great toe with the foot weight-bearing. Reproduction of heel pain along the plantar fascia origin supports the diagnosis of plantar fasciitis or heel spur-associated pain.

faqs.question: Can heel spurs come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence of symptoms after plantar fasciotomy occurs in 15-30% of patients, particularly if biomechanical, weight, or occupational factors are unaddressed. The bony spur may regrow but rarely causes recurrent symptoms when conservative measures are continued.

faqs.question: Can heel spurs cause back pain?
faqs.answer: Yes indirectly. Limping or altered gait from heel pain can produce secondary knee, hip, or back pain over time. Treating the heel pain and restoring normal gait usually resolves these compensatory complaints.
